title: Ledger OS
subtitle: A double-entry bookkeeping surface for an early-stage fintech.
summary: Replaced a 14-tab spreadsheet workflow with a single, keyboard-driven ledger that exports clean CSV. Cut month-end close from 4 days to 6 hours.
problem: The founding team was running the entire company's books in a single Notion database with 14 linked tables. Every entry required four clicks, three tab switches, and a manual currency conversion. Month-end close took four people four days, and the resulting numbers needed to be re-keyed into the actual accounting software.
process: We started with a week of contextual inquiry — sitting next to the founders while they closed the books. From there, I drafted a single-screen, keyboard-driven ledger that kept the mental model of double-entry bookkeeping but hid the ceremony. Every screen was tested against the spreadsheet, not against best practice.
solution: A single dense screen with three panels: accounts on the left, journal entries in the middle, and a running balance on the right. Everything is reachable from the keyboard. A command bar handles the 5% of operations that don't deserve a button.
results: Shipped in March 2026 to a closed beta of 12 companies. Month-end close dropped from four days to six hours. NPS is +62. The team is now expanding to invoicing and reconciliation on top of the same primitive.

title: Trace Grid
subtitle: Observability dashboard for a distributed-tracing startup.
summary: Reimagined a 7-figure-revenue tracing product around a query-first workflow. Latency to first insight: 90s → 4s.
problem: The product could answer any question about a distributed system, but only if you already knew the right query to write. New users opened the dashboard, stared at a complex form, and filed a support ticket within 90 seconds.
process: Spent two weeks shadowing the on-call rotation. Built a query-suggestion model by hand from the top 200 saved queries, then prototyped the resulting autocomplete. Validated with moderated tests against the existing form-based UI.
solution: The home screen is now a single search bar with a live waterfall preview underneath. Every query is shareable as a URL, and the most useful queries surface in a personal feed. The legacy form is still there, hidden behind a keyboard shortcut.
results: Time to first insight dropped from 90 seconds to 4. Free-to-paid conversion is up 41% quarter-over-quarter. Support volume on the dashboard dropped 58%.

title: Harbor Payments
subtitle: Cross-border payouts dashboard for marketplace operators.
summary: A payouts console that replaced five internal tools and finally made ‘where is my money?’ answerable in one screen.
problem: Marketplace operators were getting a flood of seller questions — 'where is my payout?', 'why was it delayed?', 'is it stuck?' — and the Harbor team had to dig through five different internal tools to answer each one.
process: Mapped the seller lifecycle across five tools and identified the four question shapes 80% of tickets fell into. Designed a single payouts console that could answer all four without leaving the page.
solution: A dense, table-first console with three views (by payout, by seller, by corridor) and a side panel that opens for any row. The side panel answers the four common questions inline; deeper investigation is one click away.
results: Five tools became one. The Harbor support team now resolves most payouts questions without leaving the console. The product launched to general availability in Q3 2025.

title: Lattice Docs
subtitle: API reference redesign for a developer-tools company.
summary: Rebuilt the API reference around the developer’s actual mental model: a single endpoint, with the request, the response, and the failure modes all on one page.
problem: The API reference was technically complete but practically useless: 11 tabs to read before you could make your first call, no working examples for the failure modes that mattered, and a search that returned marketing pages before docs.
process: Sat with five customer teams for two days. Rebuilt the information architecture from the request shape up, not from the URL up. Prototyped in a static HTML build that I could A/B test against the live site.
solution: A single endpoint page: request on the left, response on the right, failure modes collapsed at the bottom. Every page has a working copy-pasteable curl example. Search prioritises reference pages over marketing.
results: Time from landing on a docs page to a successful 200 response dropped 47%. Doc-search success rate is up 62%. Support tickets about the API are down a third.

title: Atlas Maps
subtitle: Field-operations app for a logistics company.
summary: A dispatch and route-confirmation app for drivers and ops. Designed for one-handed use on a glove, in the rain.
problem: Drivers were using a paper sheet and a personal phone to do a job that needed real-time dispatch updates. Ops had no visibility into what was happening on the ground.
process: Two weeks embedded with the dispatch team and the drivers. I tested paper prototypes in the cab, in the rain, with gloves on. The design rules followed from there.
solution: A two-pane mobile app: a map on the left, a tappable task list on the right. Every action is one-handed and thumb-reachable. Offline mode handles 4 hours of cell dead zones.
results: Task completion is up 28%, route deviation is down 19%, and on-shift incidents have nearly halved. The app now powers 800+ drivers across three countries.
